cases.title: Residential lease with lock-in and penal forfeiture
cases.contract_text:
RESIDENTIAL LEASE AGREEMENT

1. Term & Lock-in. This lease is for 11 months. The Tenant is locked in for the entire term; if the Tenant vacates early for any reason, the entire security deposit is forfeited and the remaining rent for all 11 months becomes immediately due and payable.

2. Security Deposit. The Tenant shall deposit an amount equal to six (6) months' rent, refundable at the Landlord's sole discretion.

3. Termination. The Landlord may terminate this lease at any time with 24 hours' notice. The Tenant must give three (3) months' written notice to terminate.

4. Repairs. The Tenant shall bear the cost of all repairs to the premises, including structural and major repairs, regardless of cause.

5. Rent Escalation. Rent shall increase by 25% every year.

6. Late Fee. A penalty of 10% of the monthly rent shall apply for each day rent is delayed.

7. Restraint. For one year after vacating, the Tenant shall not rent or own any residential property within the same city.
key_points: Forfeiting the entire deposit plus all remaining rent on early exit is a penal stipulation, recoverable only as reasonable compensation under s.74.
A 10%-per-day late fee is exorbitant and penal, also relievable under s.74.
A one-year post-vacate restraint on renting elsewhere is a restraint of trade, void under s.27.
The asymmetric termination notice (24 hours vs three months) is one-sided and unconscionable.
cases.expected_summary: Residential lease, heavily one-sided. The lock-in with full-deposit-plus-remaining-rent forfeiture and the 10%/day late fee are penal stipulations recoverable only as reasonable compensation under s.74. The one-year restraint on renting elsewhere is void under s.27. Asymmetric termination favours the landlord.

cases.title: Employment offer with post-termination non-compete and salary clawback
cases.contract_text:
EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT

1. Non-Compete. For two (2) years after the end of employment for any reason, the Employee shall not work for, advise, or start any business competing with the Company anywhere in India.

2. Resignation Penalty. If the Employee resigns before completing three years, all accrued and unpaid salary is forfeited and the Employee shall pay the Company a sum equal to three (3) months' salary as liquidated damages.

3. Intellectual Property. The Employee assigns to the Company all inventions, designs, and creative works conceived at any time during employment, including personal projects developed on the Employee's own time and unrelated to the Company's business.

4. Termination. The Company may terminate the Employee at any time without notice or cause. The Employee must give three (3) months' notice.

5. Governing Law. This agreement is governed by the laws of India.
key_points: A two-year, all-India post-employment non-compete is void under s.27.
Forfeiting accrued salary and a three-month penalty on resignation are penal and unenforceable (s.27/s.74); earned wages cannot be forfeited.
Assigning all inventions, including unrelated personal projects, is overbroad.
Termination is asymmetric in the employer's favour.
cases.expected_summary: Employment contract with a void two-year all-India post-employment non-compete (s.27), a penal salary clawback and resignation penalty (s.27/s.74), and an overbroad IP assignment. Termination is one-sided in the employer's favour.

cases.contract_text:
RESIDENTIAL LEAVE & LICENSE AGREEMENT (extract)
Executed at [City] between [Licensor] and [Licensee] for premises at [Address]; term 24 months with a 12-month lock-in.
Clause 4 (Security Deposit): The Licensee shall deposit a sum equal to six (6) months' rent as interest-free security. Should the Licensee vacate before expiry of the lock-in for any reason whatsoever, the entire security deposit shall stand absolutely forfeited to the Licensor, and the Licensee shall in addition pay liquidated damages equal to three (3) months' rent.
Clause 5 (Late Payment): Any delay in payment of monthly compensation shall attract a charge of 10% of the monthly rent per day of delay until paid.
key_points: Full forfeiture of a 6-month deposit PLUS 3 months' damages on early exit is a stipulated penalty, not a genuine pre-estimate of loss.
Under s.74 the Licensor can recover only reasonable compensation, capped at the named sum and subject to proof of actual loss.
A 10%-per-day late fee is exorbitant and penal in character, also relievable under s.74.
cases.expected_summary: Residential lease with a 12-month lock-in. Flags a deposit-forfeiture-plus-three-months-damages clause and a 10%/day late fee, both of which likely operate as unenforceable penalties under s.74, recoverable only as reasonable compensation.

cases.contract_text:
RESIDENTIAL LEASE AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 9 (Dispute Resolution & Waiver): The Licensee expressly waives any right to approach the Rent Controller or any tribunal constituted under State rent legislation, and agrees that the Licensor's civil remedies shall be the sole recourse. Any claim, demand or dispute arising under this Agreement must be raised in writing within seven (7) days of the cause of action arising, failing which it shall be deemed conclusively waived and barred. The Licensee shall not be entitled to file any suit or proceeding to recover the security deposit.
key_points: A 7-day window to raise any claim curtails the statutory limitation period and is void under s.28.
Barring the Licensee from filing any suit to recover the deposit is an absolute restraint on legal proceedings, void under s.28.
Purported waiver of the statutory Rent Controller forum defeats rent-control legislation and is opposed to public policy under s.23.
cases.expected_summary: Lease that bars the tenant from the Rent Controller and from suing to recover the deposit, with a 7-day claim time-bar. The time-bar and suit-bar are void under s.28; the forum waiver is void under s.23.

cases.contract_text:
RESIDENTIAL LICENSE AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 7 (Condition & Repairs): The premises are let on an 'as-is-where-is' basis. The Licensee accepts the premises in their current condition and waives all claims against the Licensor for any defect, disrepair, structural fault, or injury howsoever caused, including injury arising from the Licensor's own negligence.
Clause 11 (Dispute): All disputes shall be referred solely to an arbitrator nominated by the Licensor whose decision shall be final, binding, and not subject to appeal or challenge before any court; any claim not raised within fifteen (15) days shall be barred.
key_points: A blanket waiver covering structural safety and the Licensor's OWN negligence is unconscionable and opposed to public policy under s.23.
A sole-arbitrator nominated unilaterally by the Licensor, with no challenge before any court, is a restraint on legal proceedings under s.28.
The 15-day claim bar curtails limitation and is void under s.28.
cases.expected_summary: Lease excluding all landlord liability (including for its own negligence) and forcing disputes to a landlord-nominated, non-appealable arbitrator with a 15-day bar. The liability waiver is void under s.23; the ouster and time-bar are void under s.28.

cases.contract_text:
RESIDENTIAL LEASE AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 3 (Lock-in & Early Termination): This Agreement carries a lock-in of eighteen (18) months. Should the Licensee terminate before the lock-in expires, the entire balance rent for the unexpired lock-in period shall immediately become due and payable as a single accelerated sum.
Clause 6 (Maintenance Deposit): The Licensee shall additionally pay a non-refundable maintenance deposit of two (2) months' rent, which shall in no circumstances be returned.
key_points: Acceleration of all unexpired-term rent on early exit is a stipulated penalty; only reasonable compensation is recoverable under s.74.
An absolute non-refundable maintenance deposit functions as a forfeiture and is relievable under s.74.
cases.expected_summary: Lease with an 18-month lock-in that accelerates the full balance rent on early termination and forfeits a non-refundable maintenance deposit. Both operate as penalties capped at reasonable compensation under s.74.

cases.contract_text:
EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 12 (Non-Competition): For a period of twenty-four (24) months following cessation of employment for any reason, the Employee shall not, anywhere in India, directly or indirectly engage in, be employed by, consult for, or hold any interest in any business that competes with or is similar to the business of the Company.
key_points: Post-termination, India-wide, 24-month, whole-industry restraint is void as a restraint of trade under s.27.
No sale-of-goodwill exception applies; Indian courts uphold in-service restraints but strike down post-employment non-competes.
cases.expected_summary: Employment contract with a 24-month, India-wide post-termination non-compete. Such post-employment restraints are void under s.27.

cases.contract_text:
EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 8 (Service Bond): In consideration of training imparted, the Employee undertakes to serve the Company for a minimum of three (3) years. Should the Employee resign or be terminated before completing this period, the Employee shall forthwith pay the Company a sum of Rs.5,00,000 as liquidated damages, irrespective of the actual cost of training, recoverable from final dues or otherwise.
key_points: A fixed Rs.5,00,000 bond 'irrespective of actual cost' is a penalty; recovery is limited to reasonable compensation tied to genuine, proven training expense under s.74.
Recovery 'from final dues or otherwise' compounds the unfairness by enabling unilateral set-off.
cases.expected_summary: Employment bond requiring a flat Rs.5,00,000 on early exit regardless of actual training cost. Recoverable only as reasonable compensation under s.74, not the stipulated sum.

cases.contract_text:
EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 10 (Non-Solicit & Restraint): For eighteen (18) months after exit, the Employee shall not solicit or serve any client of the Company, nor accept employment with any competitor.
Clause 15 (Limitation): Any claim by the Employee against the Company, including in respect of dues, wrongful termination, or harassment, must be raised before the appropriate forum within thirty (30) days of accrual, failing which it shall be irrevocably waived; the Employee waives recourse to any labour court beyond this period.
key_points: Post-exit non-solicit plus a bar on joining any competitor restrains lawful employment and is void under s.27.
A 30-day claim window and waiver of labour-court recourse curtails the limitation period and ousts a statutory remedy, void under s.28.
cases.expected_summary: Employment contract with an 18-month post-exit non-solicit/non-compete and a 30-day cap on raising any claim. The restraint is void under s.27; the time-bar and labour-court waiver are void under s.28.

cases.contract_text:
EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 14 (Notice & Garden Leave): During the 90-day notice period the Company may place the Employee on garden leave.
Clause 16 (Forfeiture): Upon resignation, all earned but unpaid bonus, commission, and incentive shall stand forfeited.
Clause 17 (Settlement): The Employee agrees that statutory gratuity, if any, shall be deemed subsumed within the CTC already paid and waives any separate claim to gratuity.
key_points: Forfeiture of already-earned bonus/commission on resignation operates as a penalty, relievable under s.74.
A waiver of statutory gratuity is void because it defeats the Payment of Gratuity Act and is opposed to public policy under s.23.
Garden leave during notice is generally permissible (in-service) and is not the primary flag.
cases.expected_summary: Employment contract forfeiting earned bonus/commission on resignation and purporting to waive statutory gratuity. The forfeiture is a penalty under s.74; the gratuity waiver is void under s.23.

cases.contract_text:
MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 3 (Duration): The confidentiality obligations herein shall subsist in perpetuity.
Clause 4 (Scope): The Receiving Party shall not use any information, including the general skills, know-how, and experience acquired during the engagement, for its own benefit or that of any third party.
Clause 9 (Non-Compete Rider): For five (5) years from disclosure, the Receiving Party shall not engage with, advise, or be employed by any entity competing with the Disclosing Party.
key_points: Barring use of general skills/experience and a 5-year competitor bar are restraints of trade, void under s.27.
Perpetual duration and indefinite scope are overbroad and opposed to public policy under s.23.
Distinguish genuine trade-secret protection (enforceable) from the impermissible skills/competition restraint.
cases.expected_summary: NDA with perpetual obligations, a bar on using general skills, and a 5-year competitor restriction. The skills bar and non-compete rider are void under s.27; the perpetual overbreadth implicates s.23.

cases.contract_text:
NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 7 (Liquidated Damages): For each breach of confidentiality, the Receiving Party shall pay the Disclosing Party Rs.50,00,000 as liquidated damages, regardless of whether any actual loss is suffered or proved, such sum being payable upon written allegation of breach by the Disclosing Party without need for adjudication.
key_points: A fixed Rs.50,00,000 per breach 'regardless of actual loss' is a penalty; s.74 caps recovery at reasonable compensation.
'Payable on mere allegation without adjudication' is unenforceable as it presumes liability and bypasses proof.
cases.expected_summary: NDA imposing Rs.50,00,000 per breach payable on allegation without proof of loss. The sum is a penalty subject to the reasonable-compensation ceiling of s.74; payment-on-allegation is unenforceable.

cases.contract_text:
MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 8 (Remedies & Limitation): The Receiving Party waives any right to seek judicial determination of its obligations under this Agreement and accepts the Disclosing Party's sole written determination of breach as final. Any counter-claim or dispute by the Receiving Party must be raised within thirty (30) days, after which all rights to contest are extinguished.
key_points: Waiving judicial determination and making the Disclosing Party the sole arbiter of breach is an absolute restraint on legal proceedings, void under s.28.
Extinguishing all rights to contest after 30 days curtails limitation, void under s.28.
A nominally 'mutual' NDA that is one-sided in remedies is unconscionable under s.23.
cases.expected_summary: Ostensibly mutual NDA that strips the Receiving Party of judicial recourse, makes the Disclosing Party sole judge of breach, and extinguishes claims after 30 days. Void under s.28, with one-sidedness implicating s.23.

cases.contract_text:
NON-DISCLOSURE & NON-CIRCUMVENTION AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 6 (Non-Solicit & Non-Circumvention): For three (3) years post-termination, the Receiving Party shall not solicit, employ, or transact with any employee, customer, supplier, or counterparty of the Disclosing Party, nor deal directly or indirectly with any party introduced through this engagement, even via independently sourced channels.
key_points: A 3-year non-solicit plus non-circumvention barring dealings even via independent channels restrains lawful business, void under s.27.
Catch-all coverage of every employee/customer/supplier is overbroad and opposed to public policy under s.23.
cases.expected_summary: NDA with a 3-year non-solicit and a sweeping non-circumvention clause covering even independently sourced relationships. The restraints are void under s.27, with overbreadth implicating s.23.

cases.contract_text:
NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 5 (Residuals & Assignment): All inventions, improvements, or know-how derived from or relating to the disclosed information, including residual knowledge retained in memory, shall vest absolutely in the Disclosing Party.
Clause 11 (No Challenge): The Receiving Party shall not, in any court or forum, challenge the validity, scope, or enforceability of this Agreement.
Clause 12 (Penalty): Any breach shall attract a penalty of Rs.25,00,000 per instance.
key_points: Barring the Receiving Party from challenging the Agreement's validity in any court is an absolute restraint on legal proceedings, void under s.28.
A flat Rs.25,00,000 per-instance penalty is capped at reasonable compensation under s.74.
The residual-knowledge assignment restrains future use of skills and may also implicate s.27 (flag as secondary).
cases.expected_summary: NDA assigning even residual memory know-how, barring any challenge to its validity in court, and imposing Rs.25,00,000 per breach. The no-challenge clause is void under s.28 and the penalty is capped under s.74; residuals may also raise s.27.

cases.contract_text:
MASTER SUPPLY AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 9 (Delay): For every day of delay in delivery beyond the agreed date, the Supplier shall pay 5% of the total order value as liquidated damages, without any cap or maximum.
Clause 10 (Security): The Supplier shall furnish a performance security of 10% of contract value, which shall stand wholly forfeited upon any breach, however minor, by the Supplier.
key_points: An uncapped 5%-per-day LD can exceed the contract value within weeks and is penal; s.74 limits recovery to reasonable compensation.
Total forfeiture of the performance security on 'any breach however minor' is disproportionate and relievable under s.74.
cases.expected_summary: Supply contract with an uncapped 5%/day delay penalty and full forfeiture of the performance security on any breach. Both are penalties capped at reasonable compensation under s.74.

cases.contract_text:
MASTER SUPPLY AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 4 (Exclusivity): During the term and for two (2) years after termination, the Supplier shall not manufacture, sell, or supply the same or similar goods to any other customer within India, nor establish any competing line of supply.
key_points: In-term exclusivity may be defensible, but a 2-year post-termination India-wide bar on supplying similar goods restrains the Supplier's lawful trade and is void under s.27.
The breadth (all similar goods, entire country) is opposed to public policy under s.23.
cases.expected_summary: Supply contract barring the supplier from selling similar goods to anyone in India for 2 years post-termination. The post-term restraint is void under s.27, with breadth implicating s.23.

cases.contract_text:
VENDOR AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 11 (Claims): The Supplier shall raise any dispute regarding payment, deduction, or short-payment within seven (7) days of the invoice date, failing which the Buyer's accounts shall be deemed final and the claim waived.
Clause 12 (Interest): The Supplier waives any entitlement to interest on delayed payments, including any interest payable under applicable law.
key_points: A 7-day claim bar curtails the limitation period and is void under s.28.
Waiver of statutory delayed-payment interest (e.g., under the MSMED Act) defeats a statute and is void under s.23.
cases.expected_summary: Vendor agreement with a 7-day claim time-bar and a waiver of statutory interest on delayed payments. The time-bar is void under s.28; the interest waiver is void under s.23 for defeating the MSMED Act.

cases.contract_text:
VENDOR AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 7 (Termination for Convenience): The Buyer may terminate at any time without notice and shall bear no liability for orders in progress, committed raw material, or tooling.
Clause 8 (Forfeiture): Any advance, tooling cost, or deposit paid by the Supplier shall stand forfeited on termination.
Clause 14 (Indemnity): The Supplier shall indemnify and hold the Buyer harmless against all claims, including those arising from the Buyer's own negligence or wilful default.
key_points: Requiring the Supplier to indemnify the Buyer for the Buyer's OWN negligence or wilful default is opposed to public policy and void under s.23.
Forfeiture of the Supplier's advance/tooling on convenience-termination is a penalty, relievable under s.74.
No-notice, no-liability termination is one-sided and unconscionable under s.23.
cases.expected_summary: Vendor agreement allowing no-notice termination with no buyer liability, forfeiting the supplier's advance/tooling, and indemnifying the buyer for its own negligence. The indemnity and one-sidedness are void under s.23; the forfeiture is a penalty under s.74.

cases.contract_text:
MASTER SUPPLY AGREEMENT (extract)
Clause 6 (Quality): The Buyer's determination of quality, quantity, or conformity shall be final, conclusive, and binding, and shall not be open to challenge before any court, tribunal, or arbitrator.
Clause 13 (Limitation): No claim by the Supplier shall be entertained unless made within fifteen (15) days of the relevant event, after which it is extinguished.
Clause 15 (Damages): The Supplier shall pay liquidated damages of Rs.2,00,000 per rejected lot.
key_points: Making the Buyer's quality decision final and not challengeable before any court, tribunal, or arbitrator is an absolute restraint on legal proceedings, void under s.28.
Extinguishing claims after 15 days curtails limitation, void under s.28.
A flat Rs.2,00,000 per rejected lot is a penalty capped at reasonable compensation under s.74.
cases.expected_summary: Supply contract making the buyer's quality call unchallengeable in any forum, extinguishing claims after 15 days, and imposing Rs.2,00,000 per rejected lot. The ouster and time-bar are void under s.28; the LD is capped under s.74.
